For the Municipal Budget Officer, to prepare the List of Not Yet Due and Demandable Obligations at the end of the year in compliance with COA Accounting Circular Letter No. 2007-002 and for the Municipal Accountant to disclose the same in the Notes to FS.

We reiterate our previous year’s recommendations that the Local Chief Executive:

Direct the Municipal Accountant to immediately            submit the last Quarter of CY 2019 Monthly Trial Balances and Year-End FS and, henceforth, to prepare and submit the Monthly Trial Balance and Quarterly Financial Statements, with complete Schedules and Annexes within the prescribed timeframe in accordance with existing rules and regulations; 

Take appropriate immediate remedial measures, such as temporarily augmenting the Municipal Accounting Office (MAO) with personnel from other Departments, especially during report preparations, and/or allowing them to render overtime, subject to the availability of funds, in order to ensure the timely preparation and submission of the Financial Statements and reports; and,

Allow qualified plantilla personnel of the MAO to attend trainings or seminars relevant to their job assignment, to equip them with the required knowledge and skills in order to improve their performance.

We recommended Management to:

Direct the Municipal Accountant to hasten the preparation of the BRS and furnish a copy thereof to the Audit Team, together with the corresponding Journal Entry Vouchers (JEVs), paid checks and original copies of Debit and Credit Memos for review and verification; 

Make representations with the LGU’s depository bank to ensure prompt delivery of the Bank Statements. If the Banks Statements could not be released regularly by the bank at the end of each month, the Municipal Accountant may secure snapshots thereof to facilitate the preparation of the BRS; and

Take immediate remedial measures, such as augmenting the MAO with personnel from other departments, especially during report preparation and/or allowing them to render overtime services, subject to availability of funds, in order to update the submission of the BRS to meet mandated deadlines.

We recommended that Management require the OIC-Municipal Treasurer to maintain cashbooks for all bank accounts of the Municipality in order to monitor all of its cash transactions.  Further, the MTO and the MAO shall conduct reconciliation of the Cash accounts, at least on a quarterly basis, to ensure that their records are in agreement.

We recommend that the Municipal Accountant take up in the books the adjusting entries to record the unrecorded bank transactions. Henceforth, promptly prepare the BRS upon receipt of the bank statements/ snapshots thereof and record the reconciling items, if any.

We recommended that the LCE:

Supervise the LDRRM Office to achieve full implementation of the LDRRM PPAs ensuring that the expenses incurred are in accordance with the approved budget focusing on PPAs which require the highest priority in terms of urgency and impact on the four thematic areas of Disaster Risk Reduction and Management; and

Maximize the utilization of the 70% Mitigation Fund to enhance the preparedness of the Municipality against disasters and avoid, lessen or limit their adverse impacts.

We recommended that the LCE make representations with the DILG to include in its policy guidelines that a proposed project by an LGU under the LGSF-AM shall be verified first whether there is a similar project to be implemented by the National Government before approving the LGU’s proposal to avoid duplication or overlapping of projects. 

 We also recommended that the LGU request funding from the LGSF-AM for other developmental projects that it wishes to implement in the future to improve the welfare of its constituents.

We recommended that Management:

 Ensure completeness of the required supporting documents for all Contracts/POs submitted to COA for review in accordance with COA Circular 2012-001 2012 and COA Circular No. 2009-001; and

Submit immediately to the Audit Team the lacking supporting documents to the two contracts being requested for review to avoid the issuance of Notices of Suspension (NS).

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer collect the unpaid RPT in accordance with the revised real property assessments to augment the funds available to the Municipal Government for the implementation of local development programs and projects.

We also recommended that the Municipal Treasurer prepare a more realistic projection of RPT collection for the year based on prior years’ collection plus at least 10 per cent increment thereof so that Management will have a reliable basis in planning the programs and projects to be implemented in the succeeding year.

Further, the Municipal Treasurer shall send demand letters to delinquent RPT taxpayers to encourage payment thereof.

We recommended that the Municipality continue implementing GAD PPAs to attain its objective of gender equality and contribute to the empowerment of its women constituents.  Further, continue updating and maintaining the GAD Database as an effective tool to aid Management in its decision-making.

We commended the MAO and recommend that it continue its diligence in the deduction of GSIS, PhilHealth and Pag-IBIG Fund premium contributions and the remittance thereof, including the government shares, within the prescribed period.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to prepare a request for authority to write-off the dormant receivable accounts amounting to ₱2.31 million following the procedures in Sections 8.2 and 8.3 of COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

We recommended that Management require: 

The Municipal Accountant and the Property Officer to reconcile the PPELC with the RPCPPE, respectively, and immediately effect necessary adjustments, if any; and

The Municipal Engineer’s Office (MEO) to furnish the Accountant and the Supply Officer of the Inventory Report of Buildings, School Buildings, Markets, Hospitals and Health Centers and Other Structures under their custody and records to facilitate the updating and reconciliation of these assets.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant make the final reconciliation and accounting of these account balances and prepare the DVs, together with the supporting schedules, copy furnished the Auditor of the reconciled schedules and for the Municipal Treasurer to issue check payable either to the Source Agency or direct to the National Treasury. Those funds which are considered as special purpose funds shall be retained and those balances for which original releases are in the nature of financial assistance or subsidy and for which the agreement do not mention of the disposition of the unused balance, may be reverted to the unappropriated surplus of the Municipality and the future utilization thereof subject to the usual accounting, auditing and budgeting laws, rules and regulations.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant reconcile and clean the books of accounts, particularly the account Due to NGAs, in accordance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2016-005 in order to present fairly the financial statements of the Agency.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to make final reconciliation and accounting of these account balances and prepare the DVs together with the supporting schedule, copy furnished the Auditor of the reconciled schedules and for the Municipal Treasurer to issue check payable either to the source LGUs, or return to the Source Agencies. Those funds which are considered as special purpose funds shall be retained and those balances for which original releases are in the nature of financial assistance or subsidy, and for which the agreement do not mention of the disposition of the unused balance, maybe reverted to the unappropriated surplus of the Municipality; and future utilization thereof subject to the usual accounting, auditing and budgeting laws, rules and regulations.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant reconcile and clean the books of accounts, particularly the account Due to LGUs, in accordance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2016-005 in order to present fairly the financial statements of the Agency.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to:

Review the balances of payable accounts and identify the accounts with no named claimants or creditors as well as supporting documents; 

Verify accounts with identified creditors, through confirmation, whether indeed they are the valid claimants or creditors and these are supported with documents for said claims; and after which

Certify the outstanding payables, for which there are no valid claimants/creditors and no supporting documents; and 

Revert the verified amounts to the Government Equity for the General and Special Education Funds by preparing the necessary Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) as basis for recording in the books of accounts of the Municipality; and

As regards the TF accounts, return the unutilized balances to the respective Source Agencies, unless otherwise stated in the MOA, in which such amount may form part of the unappropriated surplus of the LGU.

We recommended that Management require the concerned Official or Employee in-Charge in the acceptance of deliveries to inform the Auditor of deliveries within 24 hours upon delivery, in compliance with COA Circular No. 95-006 dated May 18, 1995.

We recommended that Management implement BUB projects and programs in the most timely manner possible in order that the benefits to be derived therefrom may be enjoyed by the intended beneficiaries at the earliest possible time. We also recommended that Management secure certificates of completion and/or acceptance for the fully implemented projects in order that the corresponding fund for the project be properly adjusted and reflect the correct balance in the books.

We recommended that Management adhere strictly with the provision of Item 5.1.5 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012 and submit regularly to the COA Audit Team the Monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF, prepared by the Municipal Accountant using the proposed format by the Audit Team in “Annex B”, on or before the 15th day after the end of each month through the MDRRMC and the MDC.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Treasurer:

And other officials concerned to exert more efforts to collect the rentals from delinquent market stall occupants and if necessary avail the civil remedies provided by the present Revenue Code in order to generate additional income that will be used for the implementation of programs, projects and activities of the Municipality, and evaluate the feasibility of the continuous operations of the Market considering the situation at hand; and

To periodically monitor the list of stallholders and demand immediate payment when due and demandable to avoid arrears and accumulation of amount due.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer:
Intensify the tax collection campaign to maximize collection of RPT due to the Municipal Government in order to increase its revenue and have extra funds available to finance the delivery of basic services and facilities; and

Send demand letters to collect taxes from delinquent taxpayers and in case of failure to collect, avail of the remedies through levy on real property or judicial actions provided under the Local Government Code of 1991.

We recommended that the Sangguniang Bayan revisit/review the existing Local Revenue Code of Manito and request the Local Chief Executive to require the concerned Department Heads to prepare the proposed adjustments on tax rates as prescribed/fixed under the Code.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipality Treasurer, Municipality Assessor and other concerned Department Heads to prepare and submit to the Sangguniang Bayan for approval a proposed adjustment on tax rates to fully tap the scope of the LGU's taxing power, pursuant to the above-cited provisions of the Code in order to increase tax revenues and receipts through updating of the rates in the existing tax code which will augment the budget provision for a better delivery of government services.

We commended the Municipal Accounting Office for having faithfully remitted the mandatory GSIS, PhilHealth and Pag-IBIG Contributions and recommended to continue its diligence in the deduction of GSIS, PhilHealth and Pag-IBIG Fund premium contributions and the remittance thereof, including the government shares within the prescribed period.

We commended Management for the continued commitment in achieving desirable socio-economic development projects for the benefit of the community and its constituents. We also encouraged Management to include environmental development projects in the next Local Development Plan.
We commended Management for the continued commitment in improving their practices of proper SWM for the preservation of the environment and most importantly, ensuring the protection of public health.

We commended Management for observing and implementing a uniform rate among its JOs and COS, and we also recommended that Management revisit the provisions/stipulations of the Job Order contract and see to it that both the parties in the contract are protected.
We commended Management for actively promoting GAD related programs, projects and activities beneficial to the general welfare of all gender.

We recommended that Management ensure that Officers/Employees who perform duties requiring control, custody or possession of government funds or property be appropriately bonded in accordance with law.

We recommended that Management require the DepEd representative to furnish the LSB with the required Three-year Strategic Program and Recommendation of the DepEd PSDS and SDS prior to the preparation and approval of the Annual LSB Budget.

We recommended that Management prepare and submit the required quarterly and annual reports to the oversight department/agency in the prescribed form and within the period, and observe the posting of utilization report in the LGU website to facilitate effective monitoring of the allocation and utilization of the SEF thereby, promoting transparency and accountability.

We recommended for Management to require the Local School Board to coordinate with DepEd for the issuance of the requirements, specifications, policy and guidelines in order to ensure that the facilities and equipment, educational research, books and periodicals and sports equipment being requisitioned and procured conformed with DepEd standards pursuant to the pertinent provisions of DepEd, DBM and DILG Joint Circular (JC) No. 1, s. 2017.

We recommended that Management adopt workable strategy on how they could implement programs and projects particularly those geared towards enhancement and promotion of the general welfare of the civil society and local communities so that intended beneficiaries will be able to make use of its benefits in a timely manner.

We also recommended that the MEO prioritize those projects which are time-bounded particularly those funded out of the fund transfers from other level of the government and Management prioritize the immediate liquidation of fund releases so that succeeding releases dependent on liquidation will not be delayed.

The BAC also widen its publication of the procurement opportunity (other than posting in the PhilGEPS) by sending as many ITB/RFQ to qualified suppliers in order to solicit more participation and secure the most advantageous terms to the government.

Management also look for other potable source to augment the present water source or make some enhancements on the present water source in order to serve sufficient water to the constituents continuously

Our review of the monthly payrolls and verification of remittances to the Home Development Mutual Fund (HDMF) from January to June, 2017 disclosed that deductions representing the officials and employees’ share of the Pag-IBIG premium were based on two percent of their monthly basic salaries and not on ₱5,000 which is the prescribed maximum salary compensation provided in the afore-cited IRR.

We recommended that Management discontinue the practice of matching the employees’ share for Pag-IBIG contribution that is more than what is required by the above-cited law, to avoid said unnecessary expenditure that may deplete the funds of the LGU and which may result to audit disallowances by the Audit Team.

Verification of the implementation of infrastructure projects of the Municipality during the year based on the status report submitted as of December 31, 2017 disclosed that three projects costing ₱4.38 million were not completed on the targeted dates of completion but no liquidated damages were imposed.

We recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Engineer to strictly monitor implementation of awarded projects to ensure that these are completed as scheduled and ensure that projects are inspected on their scheduled completion dates so that percentage of unfinished work is determined as basis in the computation of liquidated damages to be deducted in the final billing. 

Further, we recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to compute and impose the liquidate damages for the afore-cited projects and collect the same from the contractors concerned and henceforth strictly comply with the above-cited provisions of the Revised IRR Annex “E” of R.A. No. 1984, in the pre-audit of payments of infrastructure projects, otherwise this warrants the issuance of audit disallowance.

Due to the absence of the Annual Procurement Plan (APP), the Municipal Government would always resort to shopping as a primary mode of procurement for goods. Since, purchases were not consolidated, the LGU is deprived of the huge discounts had these supplies, materials and equipment been purchased in bulk.  Likewise, entailing more time, effort and expenses for the preparation of these vouchers and their supporting documents when these purchases were made by every department. Clearly, the separate processing of the requests and the required documents did not support efficiency in the administration of the resources.

We recommended that, henceforth, at the start of every budget period, the end-user units of the LGU shall prepare their respective Project Procurement Management Plan (PPMP)) which shall be submitted to the Budget Office that shall evaluate each end-user’s submitted PPMP and, if warranted, include it in the budget proposal for approval. A copy of approved budget proposal and the corresponding PPMPs shall be submitted to the BAC Secretariat for its review and consolidation into the proposed APP. As soon as the Appropriation Ordinance becomes final, the end-user units shall revise and adjust the PPMP to reflect the budgetary allocation for their respective programs, activities and projects. The revised PPMP shall be submitted to the BAC, through its Secretariat, for the finalization of the modes of procurement under the proposed APP, as part of proper planning to guide the agency in choosing the proper methods of procurement, and eventually, to minimize the practice of resorting to procuring through shopping.
We recommended and Management agreed to direct the BAC, particularly the BAC Secretariat to prepare and submit a PMR to GPPB within fourteen (14) calendar days after the end of each semester.

We recommended and Management agreed to make representation with the Sangguniang Bayan for the establishment of the IAU/IAS as required under AO No. 278 and 70 to improve and strengthen the Internal Control System of the Municipality.

We recommended and Management agreed to formulate plans and projects on the utilization of its Share from National Wealth from Energy Resources in accordance with the guidelines provided under Section 2.4 of DILG and DOE Circular No. 98-01. Regarding the alleged negative balance, the OIC-Accountant should be directed to make the necessary reconciliation and adjustment of the accounts affected.

We recommended and Management agreed to require the Municipal Accountant, Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Human Resource and Management Officer to discuss and make the necessary arrangement with the Agency’s GSB for the use of ATM in the payment of salaries, wages and other benefits of the Officials and Employees of the LGU, so that government funds may be safeguarded against loss or misuse and to ensure efficiency and economy in its Cash Management.

Allow qualified plantilla personnel of the MAO to attend trainings or seminars relevant to their job assignment, to equip them with the required knowledge and skills in order to improve their performance.

Item No. 6.0 of DepEd, DBM and DILG JC No. 1, s. 2017 prescribes the Monitoring, Transparency and Accountability in the allocation and Utilization of the SEF and Item No. 6.1 specifies the Activities and Documents, Person/s Responsible and Indicative Timelines, such as in the furnishing of the SEF Budget, submission of quarterly and annual reports, and posting.

We recommended that Management prepare and submit the required quarterly and annual reports to the oversight department/agency in the prescribed form and within the period, and observe the posting of utilization report in the LGU website to facilitate effective monitoring of the allocation and utilization of the SEF thereby, promoting transparency and accountability.

We recommended that Management adopt workable strategy on how they could implement programs and projects particularly those geared towards enhancement and promotion of the general welfare of the civil society and local communities so that intended beneficiaries will be able to make use of its benefits in a timely manner.

We also recommended that the MEO prioritize those projects which are time-bounded particularly those funded out of the fund transfers from other level of the government and Management prioritize the immediate liquidation of fund releases so that succeeding releases dependent on liquidation will not be delayed.

The BAC also widen its publication of the procurement opportunity (other than posting in the PhilGEPS) by sending as many ITB/RFQ to qualified suppliers in order to solicit more participation and secure the most advantageous terms to the government.

Management also look for other potable source to augment the present water source or make some enhancements on the present water source in order to serve sufficient water to the constituents continuously

Our review of the monthly payrolls and verification of remittances to the Home Development Mutual Fund (HDMF) from January to June, 2017 disclosed that deductions representing the officials and employees’ share of the Pag-IBIG premium were based on two percent of their monthly basic salaries and not on ₱5,000 which is the prescribed maximum salary compensation provided in the afore-cited IRR.

We recommended that Management discontinue the practice of matching the employees’ share for Pag-IBIG contribution that is more than what is required by the above-cited law, to avoid said unnecessary expenditure that may deplete the funds of the LGU and which may result to audit disallowances by the Audit Team.

Verification of the implementation of infrastructure projects of the Municipality during the year based on the status report submitted as of December 31, 2017 disclosed that three projects costing ₱4.38 million were not completed on the targeted dates of completion but no liquidated damages were imposed.

We recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Engineer to strictly monitor implementation of awarded projects to ensure that these are completed as scheduled and ensure that projects are inspected on their scheduled completion dates so that percentage of unfinished work is determined as basis in the computation of liquidated damages to be deducted in the final billing. 
Further, we recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to compute and impose the liquidate damages for the afore-cited projects and collect the same from the contractors concerned and henceforth strictly comply with the above-cited provisions of theRevised IRR Annex “E” of R.A. No. 1984, in the pre-audit of payments of infrastructure projects, otherwise this warrants the issuance of audit disallowance.

Due to the absence of the Annual Procurement Plan (APP), the Municipal Government would always resort to shopping as a primary mode of procurement for goods. Since, purchases were not consolidated, the LGU is deprived of the huge discounts had these supplies, materials and equipment been purchased in bulk.  Likewise, entailing more time, effort and expenses for the preparation of these vouchers and their supporting documents when these purchases were made by every department. Clearly, the separate processing of the requests and the required documents did not support efficiency in the administration of the resources.

We recommended that, henceforth, at the start of every budget period, the end-user units of the LGU shall prepare their respective Project Procurement Management Plan (PPMP)) which shall be submitted to the Budget Office that shall evaluate each end-user’s submitted PPMP and, if warranted, include it in the budget proposal for approval. A copy of approved budget proposal and the corresponding PPMPs shall be submitted to the BAC Secretariat for its review and consolidation into the proposed APP. As soon as the Appropriation Ordinance becomes final, the end-user units shall revise and adjust the PPMP to reflect the budgetary allocation for their respective programs, activities and projects. The revised PPMP shall be submitted to the BAC, through its Secretariat, for the finalization of the modes of procurement under the proposed APP, as part of proper planning to guide the agency in choosing the proper methods of procurement, and eventually, to minimize the practice of resorting to procuring through shopping.
